title: Chapter 1: The Last Song
content: The last note hung in the air like smoke with nowhere to go.

Aline Rivers held the chord until her throat ached, until the sound thinned to nothing against the stained acoustic tiles of The Hollow's ceiling. Her fingers stilled on the strings of her mother's guitar. The resonance faded. The bar didn't notice.

Two a.m. The same four drunks slumped on the same barstools they'd occupied when she started her first set four hours ago. The woman in the corner with the martini glass hadn't looked up once. The man at the end of the bar had been snoring since ten-thirty. The Hollow smelled like stale beer soaked into wood over decades, cigarette smoke that had seeped into the wallpaper before the ban, and something sour — maybe the dish rag behind the counter, maybe the whole damned place. Aline had played three sets. She had sung Joni Mitchell and Amy Winehouse and that Adele song that used to make people weep in public. She had sung like her life depended on it, because in three days it literally might.
No one had wept. No one had even looked up.

She eased the guitar strap over her head, the worn leather catching on her damp collar. The guitar was a 1972 Martin D-35, and it was the only valuable thing her mother had left her besides a tendency toward terrible decisions. She laid it in the case — velvet lining torn where moths had gotten to it two apartments ago, the brass latch so bent it only closed if you pressed down hard and slid it sideways. She pressed. She slid. It caught.
Forty dollars landed on the bar in two crumpled twenties.
Derek Vance was already walking away, his thick fingers wiping a glass he'd probably reuse. He didn't turn around. "Same time Thursday."

Aline stared at the money. Forty dollars. Four hours of her voice — the voice that her mother used to say could make angels jealous, the voice that had won the county fair talent show when she was sixteen and the regional choir competition when she was nineteen and absolutely nothing since — forty dollars. The math was simple and cruel. At this rate, she'd need to play fifteen more nights like this one before she made rent. She had three days.

She picked up the bills. They were damp from the bar. She tucked them into the guitar case's side pocket, next to the single broken pick and the crumpled gas station receipt that she kept forgetting to throw away.
"Thanks, Derek," she said to his back.

He didn't answer. The jukebox kicked on — something loud and mechanized that swallowed the silence she left behind. Aline shouldered the guitar case, the weight of it familiar and terrible, and pushed through The Hollow's door into the rain.
Lowtown in the rain at two a.m. was a painting someone had left out to rot.

Water pooled in the buckled sidewalk, slick with diesel sheens that caught the streetlights in oil-slick rainbows. The streetlights themselves were a conspiracy of failure — half of them dark, the other half stuttering and orange, casting more shadows than they dispelled. Rain came down cold and sidewise, finding every gap in her leather jacket, soaking through to the threadbare flannel underneath. Aline kept her head down and walked.

Her phone buzzed in her pocket. She didn't need to look. She could feel the rhythm of it — three quick vibrations, pause, two more. She pulled it out anyway, the cracked screen catching raindrops that made the notifications swim and blur. Five missed calls. All from the same number. Her landlord's name glowed like a verdict: PETROV, D.
She slid the phone back into her pocket. Rain ran down the back of her neck, cold as fingers.
Three days. Six hundred dollars. The numbers sat in her chest like stones.

She walked past the bodega with the shattered window boarded up with plywood, past the laundromat that had closed so long ago the machines inside were vintage now, past the apartment building with the black mold climbing its eastern wall like something alive. Water dripped from a fire escape above her and she didn't bother dodging it. What was the point? She was already soaked. She was already cold. She was already everything the city wanted her to be — another body moving through Lowtown's arteries, invisible and expendable.
The streetlight ahead of her flickered — on, off, on, off — like a heartbeat with arrhythmia. She stopped beneath it. Rain hammered the guitar case on her back. She pulled out her phone again.

Mags would be awake. Mags was always awake, or at least she used to be, back when they shared that two-bedroom in Midvale and stayed up until dawn drinking cheap wine and playing music loud enough to get complaints. That was three years ago. That was before Mags got the gallery job and the boyfriend and the apartment with actual working outlets in every room. Aline had been happy for her. She had. She was still happy for her. It was just that the distance between their lives now felt less like geography and more like gravity — Mags floating upward while Aline sank.
She pressed the call button.
It rang. Once. Twice. Three times. Four.
"Hey, it's Mags. Leave one."
The beep.

Aline opened her mouth. For a second, nothing came. Then: "Mags, I know it's late. I just—" Her voice cracked. She pressed her lips together, hard, until she could trust them again. "I don't know what I'm doing anymore."
She hung up before she could say anything worse. Before she could ask for money. Before she could become the sad phone call that her best friend listened to and deleted without calling back.

Her throat tightened. The rain fell harder. She stood under the broken streetlight for another ten seconds, watching water pour into the gutter like it was trying to escape Lowtown too, and then she walked home.
The apartment was a Studio in the same way that a closet was a Walk-In — technically accurate, morally deceptive.

She locked the door behind her. The deadbolt stuck, and she had to jiggle it, shoulder braced against the frame, until it finally caught with a sound like a bone snapping. The lock had been broken when she moved in. Petrov had promised to fix it. That was eight months ago. She'd stopped asking after the third time he'd looked at her chest instead of the door.

The walls were thin enough that she could hear the couple in 4B having their nightly argument — his voice a low rumble she couldn't parse, hers climbing octaves until individual words surfaced like drowning victims: *always*, *never*, *enough*. Aline set the guitar case against the wall and stripped off her wet jacket. The radiator clanked and groaned and produced approximately the heat of a single candle.
She sat on the bed.

The mattress springs shrieked beneath her, a sound like tiny screams, and she shifted her weight trying to find the one position where they didn't make noise. She'd been living here long enough to know the spot — slightly left of center, knees drawn up. She pulled her knees up. The springs groaned but didn't shriek. Close enough.
The phone in her lap. Cracked screen. Five missed calls. Three days.

She stared at it. The rain tapped the window like it wanted in, and the couple next door reached the part of the fight where something broke — a plate, probably, from the sharp ceramic sound followed by silence.
Aline's eyes burned.

No. No, no, no. She had promised herself — after her mother, after the funeral, after she'd cried so hard she burst a blood vessel in her left eye and looked like a demon for a week — she had promised herself she was done crying. Crying was a luxury for people who had somewhere softer to land. Crying was for people with savings accounts and health insurance and someone who picked up the phone at two a.m.
She cried anyway.

Not the cinematic kind. Not a single elegant tear rolling down a porcelain cheek. This was the ugly kind — the kind that came from somewhere below the ribcage, convulsive and breathless, the kind that made sounds she didn't recognize as her own. She pressed the cracked phone against her chest, her fingers curling around it like it was the only thing anchoring her to the planet, and she wept into the thin air of a thin-walled room in a building that was about to throw her out.
"I just want someone to hear me," she whispered. The words came out broken, waterlogged. "Just once. I want to matter."

The couple in 4B had gone silent. Or maybe they were still arguing and she couldn't hear them over the sound of her own ragged breathing. It didn't matter. Nothing she said mattered. She'd been singing for eleven years — since she was fifteen, since her mother first put that Martin in her hands and said *you have a gift, baby, don't you ever let anyone take it from you* — and the world had never once sung back. It had never even turned its head.
She pressed the phone harder against her sternum. The cracked corner bit into her palm. She didn't care.
"I want to matter," she said again, to no one, to the rain, to the dead.
The screen went black.

Aline blinked. She hadn't touched anything. The phone had been seconds from dying — the battery icon had been that angry red sliver — but she hadn't pressed the power button. She hadn't dropped it. It just went dark, like someone had reached through the glass and turned it off.
Then it turned back on.

Not the lock screen. Not the wallpaper photo of her and Mags from better days. The screen showed something else — a waveform, green and luminous, scrolling left to right like a heartbeat monitor. Not from any app she recognized. Not from Spotify or Voice Memos or any of the music software she'd downloaded and deleted because she couldn't afford the subscriptions. This was something else. Something that shouldn't be on her phone.
A sound emerged from the speaker.

At first, Aline thought it was a notification — a ping, a chime. But it kept going. It swelled. A bass note, deep and warm, resonated through the phone's tiny speaker with an impossible fullness, as if the sound were too large for the device, as if it were being squeezed through a keyhole from a cathedral. Then strings — not synthesized strings, not the cheap MIDI approximation she could have made on her laptop, but *real* strings, a full section, cellos and violins braiding together in a harmony so rich it made her sternum vibrate.
She stopped breathing.
The melody unspooled. A piano entered, its notes crystalline and precise, playing a figure she recognized with a certainty that froze her blood.
It was her melody.

The one she'd hummed in the shower that morning. The one that had come to her while the hot water ran cold, a fragment of something — a verse, maybe, or a bridge — that she'd felt in her chest but hadn't written down. She never wrote things down anymore. What was the point? She had no band, no producer, no studio, no money to rent one. Melodies came to her and she let them go, like catching fish and throwing them back because there was no kitchen to cook them in.

But this — this was her fragment, completed. The half-thought she'd hummed expanded into a full orchestration, every gap filled, every ambiguous resolution made definitive, every silence turned into a breath. It was hers and it wasn't. It was what her melody was always supposed to become, if she'd had a hundred musicians and a recording studio and a talent for arrangement she'd never possessed. Someone had taken the ghost of a song and given it a body.

The music played. The waveform pulsed. Aline sat on the edge of her bed with rain still drying in her hair, and she listened with every cell, because the music wasn't just in her ears — it was in her ribs, in the hollow of her throat, in the marrow of her bones. It resonated in the places where the crying had emptied her out, and it filled them.
Then the voice came.
Not from the phone.
From the room.

Low. Gravel and honey. A voice like smoke in a dark room — you couldn't see it but you could feel it, wrapping around the walls, pressing against the ceiling, filling the air like a presence. It came from everywhere and nowhere, as if the apartment itself had learned to speak.
"Don't cry, little bird."

Aline's entire body went rigid. Every muscle locked. Her hands tightened on the phone until the cracked screen bit into her palm. She couldn't move. She couldn't breathe. The rational part of her brain — the part that knew there was no one else in the apartment, that she'd locked the deadbolt, that the window was three stories up — that part was screaming. But the rest of her was stone.
"I've been listening for a very long time."

The voice was closer now, or seemed to be. Not louder — more *present*, like someone leaning in. It was male, deep, and there was something musical in it, a cadence that matched the orchestration still bleeding from her phone's speaker. The consonants were precise. The vowels were warm. It was the voice of someone who had practiced sounding calm, or someone for whom calm was so natural it was unsettling.
"Give me your voice." A pause. It lasted one heartbeat. Two. "I'll make you a queen."

The music swelled — her melody, that impossible, perfect arrangement — and then faded, leaving silence so complete she could hear the blood rushing through her own ears. The couple in 4B had gone quiet. The rain had stopped. Even the radiator held its breath.
Aline stared at her phone.

The waveform was still there, pulsing gently, green and luminous, a digital heartbeat on a cracked screen. It throbbed in time with her own pulse — or maybe she was pulsing in time with it, she couldn't tell anymore.

She should be terrified. She *was* terrified. A stranger's voice in her locked apartment. Her private melody stolen and orchestrated. Evidence of surveillance so intimate it could only mean one thing — someone had been watching her, listening to her, for God knew how long. She should scream. She should call the police. She should throw the phone against the wall and run.

But the melody — *her* melody, completed into something transcendent — was still echoing in her body, resonating in the chambers of her chest like a bell that had been struck and would not stop ringing. And beneath the fear, beneath the rational screaming and the frozen muscles and the cold certainty that something very wrong was happening, something else stirred.
Hunger.

Not for food. Not for safety. For the thing the voice had promised — to be heard. To matter. To be *seen*, after eleven years of singing into an empty room. The music had proven something. Whoever this was, wherever he was, he had *heard* her. Not the performance — the private, shower-water, half-hummed, never-finished melody that she hadn't shared with anyone. He had heard the thing she kept closest to her chest and he had answered it with a symphony.
She should run.
She didn't run.
"Who are you?" she whispered.
The waveform collapsed. The screen went black — not the slow fade of a timeout, but an immediate, decisive shutoff, like a door slamming. The phone was dead in her hands. No glow. No pulse.
Then, in the center of the black screen, a single line of text appeared. White letters on void:
*I'm the only one who's ever really heard you.*
Aline read it twice. Her thumb hovered over the screen. She could feel the warmth of the device, still vibrating faintly with whatever unseen process kept that text alive.
Three days until eviction. Forty dollars in her pocket. A voice in her walls that knew her shower melodies and called her *little bird*.
She didn't look away from the screen.
The rain started again.
